data.title: Geopolitical Environment Supports Oil As Weather Sends Gas Soaring
data.description:
By Warren Patterson, Head of Commodities Strategy

Oil up for now but surplus set to weigh on prices

Oil prices have held up well so far this year, with several geopolitical events providing support to the market. The US arrest of Venezuela’s president and escalating tensions between the US and Iran pose risks to supply, particularly with the latter. An escalation between the US and Iran puts around 1.5m b/d of Iranian oil exports at risk – but clearly, any escalation would also raise concerns over broader Persian Gulf oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz, where roughly 20m b/d moves through.

There have also been disruptions to oil supply elsewhere. This has proved supportive for the prompt Brent timespread, which has strengthened this month. Kazakh oil exports from the CPC terminal in Russia have been under pressure following drone attacks on the terminal, while oil production from the country has also faced unplanned outages due to power issues. Still, these flows are set to recover in the weeks ahead. Meanwhile, freezing weather conditions in the US not only provide support to demand for heating fuels but also pose a risk to some US oil production and refinery operations.

However, the scale of the expected oil surplus in the market suggests that if and when geopolitical risks ease, we should see oil prices coming under pressure. We continue to hold onto the view that ICE Brent will average $57/bbl over 2026. A weakening in timespreads would make us even more confident in the view of lower oil prices.

Natural gas prices surge on US deep freeze

Natural gas prices have seen significant strength through January. Initially, strength was seen in the European market, with colder weather, tighter storage, and speculative short-covering providing a boost to TTF.

However, more recently, the US gas market has dominated market moves, with a severe winter storm across the US raising heating demand and leading to production shut-ins. This saw Henry Hub front-month futures more than double in as little as 10 days, trading to their highest levels since 2022. Speculators were also caught off guard, holding a net short in Henry Hub ahead of the storm. Short covering from speculators would have only added fuel to the fire. The impact of the storm is likely to lead to a significant drawdown in US natural gas storage, but given that storage was very comfortable ahead of the storm, it should be manageable; we expect that prices will correct lower, assuming no longer-term impact on supply from the storm.

The US winter storm has raised further supply concerns for Europe, with US LNG plants having reduced operating rates, suggesting that US LNG flows to Europe could slow. This has led to European gas prices trading at a large premium to Asia to ensure LNG cargoes are directed to Europe.

It is looking increasingly likely that EU gas storage will finish the 2025/26 heating season below 2022 levels. However, the difference between 2022 and 2026 is that now we have a sizeable amount of LNG export capacity ramping up, which should ease supply concerns in the medium to long term. In addition, there is much less uncertainty over Russian gas flows to Europe in 2026 than in 2022.

We retain our forecasts for TTF to average EUR33/MWh over the first quarter of 2026 and EUR30/MWh over the full year 2026.

EU gas storage tighter than usual (% full)

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

data.title: OZEM Vs. THNR: Which Is The Better ETF In The GLP-1 And Weight Loss Space?
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I recommend buying the Roundhill GLP-1 & Weight Loss ETF (NASDAQ:OZEM). This is my first article about a thematic ETF of this type. However some time ago I wrote my investment thesis about Eli Lilly, and the company was part of my recommended stock portfolio for 2025. In this article my intention is to analyze the characteristics of OZEM, in addition to analyzing the growth of the sector and the true catalyst that makes the thesis so attractive in my opinion.

Context

On September 4, I wrote an article about Eli Lilly. The title of the article was: Eli Lilly's Stock Fall Is An Opportunity. Since then, the total return is 39.25% while the S&P 500 is 8.08%. I was so convinced of the company's thesis that it was even part of my recommended stock portfolio in 2025, which even handily beat the S&P 500.

For 2026, I made adjustments to my recommended stock portfolio and included a mix of American stocks with Brazilian stocks, aiming for a higher dividend payment and exposure to emerging markets with cheap valuations.

Therefore, Eli Lilly left my portfolio. Another major issue in the investment thesis is that Eli Lilly, despite being in an extremely promising sector, had a much higher valuation than its competitors. All of these questions made me look for a new way of gaining exposure to the weight loss drug industry. Therefore, today, I'm going to talk about OZEM.

Roundhill GLP-1 & Weight Loss ETF

If you have ever wondered which is the best pharmaceutical company to invest in, be it the owner of Mounjaro or the owner of Ozempic or any other pharmaceutical company with weight loss drugs, the ETF I will mention will probably solve your problem.

Today I'm going to talk about OZEM. It is managed by Roundhill Investments and was created in 2024. It currently has $58 million in AUM and an expense ratio of 0.59%. Its main position is Novo Nordisk with 22.59%, its second largest position is Elly Lily with 14.36%, and its third largest position is Structured Therapeutics with 4.88%. Its 10 largest holdings represent 68.79% of the portfolio which is made up of a total of 28 holdings.

It is worth noting that OZEM does not have a benchmark, as the manager actively manages the portfolio. Its main peers are the Amplify Weight Loss Drug & Treatment ETF (THNR) which has an AUM of $3.92 million and a 0.59% expense ratio, and the Therma Obesity & Cardiometabolic ETF (HRTS) which has $56 million in AUM and a 0.75% expense ratio. Since its creation, OZEM has performed much better than its peers accumulating an increase of more than 42%.

It is curious to observe the discrepancy in the return of OZEM and THNR, given that both have a very similar portfolio composition, especially as THNR has a smaller weight than Novo Nordisk, which had a performance of -29% in the last 12 months. Why does this occur?

Well, as stated, OZEM has active management, while THNR tracks the VettaFi Weight Loss Drug & Treatment Index, which is why THNR has exposure to more solid companies such as Roche, Merck, Astrazeneca and Pfizer.

On the other hand, OZEM has exposure to high-growth companies. As an example, Structure Therapeutics shares are up 183% in the last 12 months and Ascletis Pharma shares are up 633% in the last 12 months.

Which should you choose? As my objective is to capture gains from the obesity and GLP-1 theme, my option is OZEM, however, if you are a conservative investor but still want exposure to this theme, you should choose THNR.

Growth

Market research indicated that the global GLP-1 agonists weight loss drugs market was $13.84 billion in 2024 and is projected to grow at a CAGR of 18.5% from 2025 to 2030 to reach $48.84 billion. Therefore, investing in OZEM is strategic in my opinion.

The True Catalyst

The effectiveness of the medicines is shown in robust pharmaceutical tests, however, it is necessary to highlight that if treatments with weight loss pens have already been in such demand, imagine the prospects of creating oral medicines.

This is the argument used by most analysts, which could be a trigger for an even greater adherence to GLP-1 agonists weight loss drugs. However, I go further, as it seems to me that the triggers for the sector are much more linked to new discoveries.

In this sense, recently the companies Nvidia and Eli Lilly announced an investment of $1 billion to build and jointly operate a new laboratory that will use artificial intelligence to develop new medicines.

The great ambition is to deliver a pioneering project in robotics and physical AI to accelerate the production of pharmaceuticals. According to Nvidia, the laboratory will enable AI-assisted experiments and will work around the clock, seven days a week.

Data processing and application of models will be done using the BioNeMo platform, created by Nvidia for pharmaceutical research laboratories. The infrastructure will run on Vera Rubin family racks, a set of Rubin GPUs and Vera CPUs.

It is worth noting that the initiative is in line with projects such as Google DeepMind, which used AI science to design the revolutionary AlphaFold, a series of algorithms used to discover proteins and simulate the interaction of molecules. The year 2026 will be marked by robust gains in scale for companies using AI, in my opinion, and the healthcare sector stands to be one of the sectors that benefits the most

Valuation

Another great attraction of OZEM is its valuation weighted by its holdings. While Lilly trades at 45x earnings, OZEM trades at 34x earnings and has several investment theses in this promising sector.

It is curious that OZEM has already traded at 55x profits, and it seems to me that the trigger for it to return to these levels is the success of sales of oral GLP-1 agonists weight loss drugs. In this case, the upside is 22%, and this corroborates my buy recommendation.

Potential Threats To The Thesis

Unlike sectors such as utilities, which are characterized by predictability, the same does not occur with the healthcare sector, especially pharmaceutical companies such as Eli Lilly and Novo Nordisk. This is because any side effects to your medications can cause a sharp drop in sales. Likewise, launch failures can also wipe out millions of dollars in development investments.

Therefore, this is not an investment recommended for conservative investors, especially in the context of artificial intelligence that I mentioned. The fact that companies explore more AI in their businesses will add additional volatility to the case, which may not be so interesting given the high valuation multiples.

The Bottom Line

I have always been very convinced of the investment thesis of Eli Lilly and the weight loss medicine sector. The company was actually part of my recommended stock portfolio in 2025. However, market conditions made me change that portfolio and remove the company.

However, I remained convinced of the investment thesis and chose to find other alternatives to invest in the sector. So, I found an ETF that is little talked about by analysts, which is OZEM, which basically consolidates the main companies in the sector.

The prospects are very attractive, especially in the context of the use of artificial intelligence, and the valuation of OZEM is also attractive in my view, given the prospects for medicines and the population's awareness of weight loss and health gains.

Based on this analysis, I reiterate the recommendation to buy OZEM. In my view, the risk-return relationship on the investment is quite attractive, given the prospects and valuation.

data.title: Wise: Overvalued On Declining Margin Strategy
data.description:
Introduction

In December 2025, I initially covered Wise (WPLCF) and rated it a Sell. The SA community largely disagreed with my analysis and view, citing the large TAM (total addressable market) and Wise's competitive advantages as a first mover in the international money transfer sector. The fiscal 3Q26 trading update (as a UK company, they do not have mandatory quarterly results) led to a rally as Wise reported a 25% gain in TPV (total payment volume) and a lower-than-expected reduction in the take rate. However, the most recent consensus shows only a modest upgrade in earnings, but the long-term dynamics remain unchanged. Wise is a volume-driven business that needs to continue cutting fees to deliver 15% earnings growth, and its shares are overpriced relative to fintech peers.

Consensus Upgrade

Post fiscal 3Q26 operating data (calendar December 2025), the shares rallied over 20%, but the consensus earnings for YE25 nudged up 5% and for YE26, 4%. However, the sharp downward trend in net margins remains the tonic. The analysts contributing to the consensus are not inventing numbers; their forecasts are based on company guidance. Wise has a strategy that involves charging lower fees to build volume, which translates into modest earnings growth. Note that the YE25 data is Wise's fiscal YE26, which ends in March 2026.

The Wise Platform

Wise sells its network capacity to other financial institutions, collecting a fee but not interacting with the end customer. I believe the strategy here is to become the Visa/Mastercard of cross-border money transfer, and by providing other banks/FinTechs with a low-cost, efficient platform, they will not seek to build their own networks. Hence, Wise is creating a significant moat but at the expense of medium-term margins and growth. This implementation may take several years and lower net fees as the mix shifts from predominantly retail to the platform. It's estimated that about 50% of business TPV is now this institutional platform flow. Wise may then be able to convert TPV into stable margins at a solid growth rate.

Operating Model

The operating model estimates have not changed much from my December report; margins are up about 1% on a slower pace of fee reductions, but the trend is still down as Wise looks to “pass on” savings from adding its own network vs. using corresponding banks. At the same time, a sizeable chunk of business TPV comes from the platform, which also reduces fees or revenue per customer. The chart below shows that total revenue per TPV may fall from £0.10 (pence) to £0.08, and EBITDA declines to £0.02 by 2028. This steep drop from the YE23 high is part of the reason why the shares have underperformed.

Peer Comps

Part of the reason for a negative view of Wise shares is that the peer group of fintechs is substantially lower valued than Wise, with higher cash earnings growth (net income, depreciation, and stock-based compensation). It's true that few focus on cross-border money transfers, but the market views this sector as a single one, whether for payments or pay later. What is important is growth, profitability, and the ability to displace legacy financials. Wise is expensive at 1.2x PEG, compared with the sector average of 0.6x.

Valuation

I continue to see downside to the Wise shares on current estimates and sector valuations. The consensus price target has not changed, and the analysts still value the stock at almost 24x P/CE (cash earnings) or 1.8x PEG, which I find expensive. My GBP7.5 price target is nearly unchanged vs. the December report and has even more downside after the recent rally.

Risk

Regulation may be Wise's greatest risk: if it loses banking or financial transaction licenses across its network, it may cease generating revenue. Money laundering, tax regulations, and sanctions pose operating risks. Competition is another risk factor that can hurt TPV and margins, which is perhaps why Wise is focused on customer service at the expense of margins. Stablecoins and decentralized finance could pose risks, but I believe Wise may opt to accept stablecoins as a currency on its network, subject to regulations.

Conclusion

I rate Wise a Sell. The stock is not cheap, and growth is unlikely to accelerate given the company's strategy of fee reductions for retail and the expansion of its institutional platform, both of which are positive for TPV and operating resilience but are overpriced in my view. A US listing may not provide greater valuation, and I would argue could hurt them as more investors compare Wise to the cheaper fintech sector.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: FLEX LNG: 11% Yield On LNG Shipping
data.description:
We previously covered Flex LNG Ltd.(FLNG) in a June 2025 article, in which we found that "FLNG's strengths continue to earn it premium valuations vs. its industry." We recommended holding off on buying new shares at the time, and waiting for FLNG to have lower valuations vs. its industry.

Company Profile:

"Flex LNG Ltd. is a shipping company focused on the growing market for Liquefied Natural Gas (LNG). Our fleet consists of thirteen LNG carriers on the water and all of our vessels are state-of-the-art ships with the latest generation two-stroke propulsion." (FLNG site.)

Fleet:

FLNG's fleet now consists of these 13 vessels, mostly on mid- to long-term contracts. FLNG's fleet has "53 years of minimum firm backlog which may grow to 80 years with charterers’ extension options." (FLNG site.)

Earnings:

As detailed in the chart below, spot rates were higher in 2024 than in 2025, particularly in the 2nd half, when they remained well above average. However, spot rates did start to ramp up in Q4 '25 as the winter season came on:

Q3 '25: After a slow 1st half, FLNG had a stronger Q3 '25. It also completed the drydocking of 2 vessels, Flex Artemis and Flex Amber, while 2 earlier drydocking vessels came back into service. Interest expense continued to improve, falling 32%, or $11M in the quarter.

Time Charter Equivalent "TCE" was ~$70,900/day, a bit lower than management's full year 2025 guidance of $71,500/day.

Looking back further, Q1-3 '25 revenue was down slightly, at 2%, while Net Income fell by 26.5%, due in part to multiple drydockings in 2025. Adjusted Diluted EPS fell by ~20%, while Adjusted EBITDA was down by ~7%. Interest expense improved by ~$10M, or 13%, and the share count was stable.

Full Year 2025 Guidance:

Management's full year 2025 guidance implies negative growth for 2025 vs. 2024, with Revenue expected to be ~$340M, 4.6% lower; Adjusted EBITDA forecast at ~$250M, 8.4% lower; and TCE/Day at $71.50/day, 4.5% lower than in 2024:

Industry Trends:

The US was the top LNG exporter in Q1-3 '25, up 22% in tonnage of 87.3 metric tons, followed by Qater, up 3% at ~68MT, and Australia, at 62.2 MT, down 5%.

On the import side, Indonesia led, with 113.6 metric tons, down 1%; followed by Europe, at 102.6 MT, up 26%. China, however, decreased by 18%, importing 53.5 MT, as did India, which dropped by 6%, for 21.3 MT:

North America is expected to lead the way in LNG capacity growth in 2026-2028, followed by Qatar:

There is also a large orderbook for new LNG vessels, however, most of these new vessels are already committed to charters - less than 30 are available.

Dividends:

At $26.23, FLNG yields 11.44%. It has paid $.75/quarter since Q4 2023. It should go ex-dividend next on ~2/20/26, with a ~3/6/26 pay date.

FLNG paid $121.70M in dividends in Q1-3 '25, while its Operating Cash Flow, (which includes drydocking expenses), was less, at $104.31M, so the coverage factor was below 1X, at .86X. Full year 2024 saw the best coverage factor over the past 2+ years, of 1.13X:

Profitability & Leverage:

ROA and ROE slipped a bit, with ROE remaining above industry averages. Leverage increased, and was much higher than industry averages. Interest coverage improved slightly, but remained lower than the industry average. EBITDA Margin was lower, but it was much higher than the industry average:

Debt & Liquidity:

FLNG has no debt maturity prior to 2029, when its $290M credit facility, and its $150M credit facility on the Flex Enterprise vessel come due.

FLNG refinanced of Flex Resolute and Flex Constellation vessels in Q3 '25, which resulted in an all-time high cash balance of $479M, as of 9/30/25.

Performance:

FLNG has lagged its industry by a wide margin over the past year and so far in 2026. It has also lagged it over the past month and quarter, but it has outperformed the S&P 500. FLNG's high dividend yield made its 15.35% 1-year total return very close to the S&P's 15.67% total return.

Risks: The marine shipping industry can be very volatile, as spot rates are very vulnerable to political and macroeconomic events. A prolonged trade war or worldwide recession could negatively demand. Fortunately for FLNG, it has mostly long term charters, with 53 years of minimum firm backlog for its fleet.

Analysts' Price Targets:

At $26.23, FLNG is ~even with analysts' average price target, and 6.7% below their $28.11 highest price target.

Valuations:

FLNG looks undervalued on a trailing and forward P/E basis, whereas it's still receiving premium Price/Sales, Price/Book, and EV/EBITDA valuations vs. its industry. That support is most likely linked to its much higher than average dividend yield.

Parting Thoughts:

FLNG is only ~4% below its 52-week high of $27.67, but over 36% above its 52-week low of $19.46, and is much closer to being overbought than oversold on its slow stochastic chart. We suggest holding off, and waiting for a lower entry price.

All tables furnished by Hidden Dividend Stocks Plus, unless otherwise noted.

data.title: HubSpot: Out Of Favor, But Staying Bullish
data.description:
Like many software companies, HubSpot (HUBS) has been at the center of debate around whether or not AI technology will completely disrupt the need for traditional software vendors. There has been a complete meltdown across the sector, and HUBS has been one of the most impacted stocks.

HUBS is one of the leading customer relationship management systems for SMBs and mid-market companies, providing software across marketing, sales, customer service, and website management. For smaller companies, this can be the ultimate one-stop shop to help run the business, essentially Salesforce but at a much smaller scale.

The stock is now down 65% from its 2025 peak, including a 30% pullback in 2026 alone. With very little company-specific catalyst to drive the stock lower, the clear culprit is investor fears that AI will take over the world.

Of course, this is a very draconian view of what could happen to software, but the advancements across the LLM models, such as OpenAI and Anthropic have been impressive. Whether is was OpenAI's Developer Day announcements or Anthropic's introduction of Claude Cowork, there is an immense amount of fear that these AI technologies will render traditional software applications as legacy tech.

HUBS, in many ways, is being viewed as the prime example of a company that can be disrupted. Across the SMB and mid-market space, there are many opportunities for more efficient technology to disrupt and replace traditional methods of getting work done. And given the low barriers to entry to adopt the new AI technology, SMBs could be a prime example of companies willing to try it out.

Of course, HUBS does have many AI features embedded within its product suite. But the argument is that these LLMs and “vibe coding” will make spinning up new business applications much easier and cheaper than ever before. Why pay for an expensive piece of software when you can easily build and customize it yourself. That is the promise AI wishes to fulfill.

While I believe there are many risks to HUBS business model, the ongoing stock correction should encapsulate most of this by now. As discussed more below, HUBS remains in a formidable position in the market, and absent the doomsday scenario where AI takes over all of software, I believe this continued pullback in shares is a great buying opportunity.

Financial Recap

HUBS reports its Q4 quarter on February 11, and this could very well be the most important quarter in the company's history. Given the fragility witnessed so far during earnings season, companies are being severely punished for missing—or even just meeting—key metrics.

Looking at the company's Q3 report, revenue grew 21% yoy to $809.5 million and beat consensus estimates by ~3%. Even this magnitude of a beat was not good enough to get the stock going.

Without retroactively analyzing last quarter, I believe it's very important to point out that HUBS is growing 20%+, maintained gross margin ~85%, and has expanded operating margins to 20%+. These are signs of a strong underlying business, and the margin expansion trends could imply more to come.

The one area that will be closely followed is the billings trend. This is often viewed as one of the best leading indicators for software companies. In Q3, billings growth was 18% yoy, or 19% yoy excluding the currency headwind. Could this be an indication that growth is going to slow? Potentially.

In fact, the company's guidance for Q4 is for revenue growth of 18%, or just 16% on a currency-neutral basis. Even if HUBS is able to beat the guide by a few points, as they typically do, this could still represent stable/decelerating growth. Given how volatile the market has been, this might not be good enough. However, and as discussed more during the valuation section, investors have already heavily discounted the stock since the Q3 report.

Expectations should be reset much lower, or at least the stock might not be punished as much if the 2026 guidance comes in a little light.

According to Yahoo Finance, consensus estimates 2026 revenue growth to be 16% yoy, representing several points of deceleration relative to the ~18.5% yoy growth expected in 2025.

In my opinion, the stock is not currently pricing in a company growing 16% with 20%+ operating margin. Rather, the stock is pricing in a much worse deterioration in revenue, ultimately driven be fears around AI.

HUBS' management team presented at several investor conferences in December, noting their confidence in the underlying business trends. When asked about growth trends, management noted the following.

And I do think that AI is a multiple year tailwind, and some of the pricing changes that we have made also aids that tailwind. We're not ready to give 2026 guidance. I have Chuck [CFO] somewhere here, and he will literally stare me down. So I'm not here to give any guidance in terms of 2026, but our aspiration is to grow faster than where we are today.

During a separate conference, the management team also discussed the broader impacts of LLMs on the competitive environment.

Any new technology wave is going to change the competitive landscape. The value that LLMs provide, let's just assume that it is going to continue to increase, which means the value that we provide on top of LLM has to continue to increase. It will attract new entrants. And when I look at like HubSpot and how we plan to compete from the product perspective, our strategy is to ensure that we are delivering value on top of what AI can do. We really look at AI and LLMs being complementary to what the capabilities of HubSpot are. And so our strategy is to provide value on top of what LLMs provide and be exceptionally complementary.

Now, part of management's job is to remain bullish about the long-term view of the company. So everything should be ready with a grain of salt. However, it was very encouraging to read that the CEO believes the company's growth can actually accelerate from current levels. Obviously, the market is heavily disagreeing with this, which is why the upcoming earnings report will be significant.

If 2026 guidance comes in better than consensus estimates, this could revive some life back into the stock. If its worse than expectations, then watch out below.

Valuation

In a volatile market where software companies are heavily out of favor, valuation becomes almost irrelevant. Just because a stock is currently cheap, even at all-time low multiples, does not mean that things can't get worse.

HUBS is currently trading at ~4.3x forward revenue and ~24.6x forward EPS. Given the company's growth profiles and profit margins—and assuming a normalized investing environment—these would be discounted multiples.

However, the current market paradigm is causing software companies to be heavily discounted. And while ~4.3x revenue is the lowest point for HUBS, this multiple could still see more compression. If growth were to decelerate towards 10%, the stock's multiple would likely be cut in half.

To be clear, I do not believe that is the case. The market is already heavily discounting HUBS' revenue growth profile. With the stock experiencing a 65% pullback over the past year, I believe this has drastically reduced growth expectations. Absent a completely whiff with the 2026 guidance, I believe the next report could be the final clearing event before buyers start to return.

Risks

The biggest risk to my bullish thesis is that AI technology, including those being developed by the leading LLMs, starts to win business held by current application vendors. If HUBS were to start seeing direct competitive pressure from these LLMs, investors would likely stay away from the stock. In some ways, the market is already pricing this in. However, if more tangible signs were to demonstrate this is actually happening, the stock could go much lower.

Final Thoughts

Catching a falling knife is dangerous. With HUBS, the company offers a low-cost software offering to a large customer base. The management team remains confident that AI innovation can actually drive growth and accelerate revenue, but that has yet to be seen.

The market has materially discounted valuation over the past several months, which in my opinion, has essentially eliminated all expectations heading into the next quarterly report. If HUBS is able to provide guidance that meets expectations, the stock could start working from here.

While there is still downside risk, I believe the balance skews in favor of the upside. Cautiously optimistic about shares at current levels and believe the upside reward here can be massive if 2026 guidance impresses.

data.title: Boeing Is Flying Steady Into 2026
data.description:
Boeing's (BA) stock in the last 12 months is up 38.9% while the S&P is up 14.4%. While this is a very good one-year return, there is a lot more to this story than meets the eye.

Boeing obviously has had numerous issues in recent years that they have had to overcome. Mass loss of lives from planes going down, claims of sub-par materials and poor quality control, along with a door flying off in mid-air over Portland shortly after take off, have greatly damaged their reputation not only with air travelers, but with investors in the stocks.

In other words, it has been a very rocky decade for the company, which has also led to a very volatile ride for shareholders in the company. In this article, we will take a look at the past and recent performance of the shares and then do a five-year valuation going forward based on the numbers that are out there on the street right now.

This is the same process that I put all stocks through in my very demanding and stringent investment stock selection process.

On my Tuesday, Jan. 26, edition of my daily radio show/podcast, 'Best Stocks Now', I discussed whether or not Boeing (BA) is worthy of a spot amongst the 22 other stocks that we currently own in our premier growth portfolio. This is one of four portfolios that I manage and mirror here in Seeking Alpha with my premium service.

The other three portfolios are Ultra Growth, Emerging Growth, and Value/Relative Value.

Our cash position right now in the Premier Growth portfolio is currently at about 25%. This is not because we are bearish, but because we are picky! I currently have 7-8 vacant slots to finish filling up this 7 year old portfolio. Does Boeing currently meet my very stringent criteria?

Boeing, one of the thirty stocks in the Dow Jones Industrial Average, reported their quarterly earnings on Tuesday. As you can see from the chart below, this is one of their best quarters in a long, long time.

BA's earnings in Q4 look great on paper, with $9.92 per share in earnings and a 57% jump in sales. But, one of the reasons for that explosion of profit, or at least a large part of it, is that it sold a big piece of its business.

On April 22nd of last year, 2025, Boeing sold part of its Digital Aviation Solutions unit. That sale alone created a one-time profit of $9.6 billion. This is the main reason that Q4 looks so good on paper.

Now, that's not to say the core business isn't getting better. Even ignoring the one-time sale, Boeing delivered 160 airplanes this quarter. That is 103 more than last year. Their revenue jumped 57% year over year, and their cash flow has finally turned positive once again.

However, Boeing still has some real issues to work through. Their commercial airplane business might be full, but they're still losing money. Defense programs, especially tankers, had losses, and Boeing still carries $54 billion in debt!

Despite the issues though, Boeing is healing. In August 2024, they replaced long-time CEO Dave Calhoun with Kelly Ortberg. While Calhoun had led Boeing through a very turbulent time filled with pandemic impacts, intensified regulatory scrutiny, and an abundance of 737 MAX safety issues; the culture of Boeing under his leadership was less connected to the reality of manufacturing and more driven towards temporary, often dangerous, gains.

Ortberg, however, has emphasized engineering excellence, quality control, and operational stability over short-term earnings. The shift towards a new hands-on engineering and operations-focused Boeing can be seen quite literally, as the CEO's office was relocated back to the Boeing production heartland of Seattle.

Now for some basic facts about the company

Data from Best Stock Now App

Boeing currently has a market-cap of almost $195 billion. It is a large-cap stock with a moderate to conservative risk profile (although it has carried a lot more risk than that in recent years Also, keep in mind that they still have a lot of liability out there to work their way through.

My stock analysis process always begins with looking at the track record of the shares and comparing it against the other 5,193 stocks, ETFs, and mutual funds in my proprietary database.

Data from Best Stocks Now App

As you can see from the screenshot above BA's stock is up 38.9% over the last twelve months, while the S&P is up 14.4%. The stock has delivered some alpha lately, but we need to look at this short-term performance in context with its longer-term track record.

The stock has delivered an average annual total return of just 4.76% over the last three years that compares with an average annual return of 24% per year for the S&P 500. That meager return has come along with a lot of volatility and a lot of risk.

Over the last five years, the stock has delivered an average annual total return of just 3.89% vs. an average annual return of 16.3% for the S&P 500. Over the last ten years, the stock has rewarded investors with an average annual return of 7.86% while the much less volatile S&P 500 has returned an outsized average of 26.7%.

What a decade for the market it has been! At the same time, it has been a very volatile and weak decade for Boeing.

But that's all looking in the rear-view mirror. When I'm deciding what stocks to put into my portfolios, I also always look at a five-year forward valuation. That is looking forward. This valuation is critical to me, and honestly the Seattle-based company's current valuation doesn't look like it's making it to the Super Bowl like the Seahawks are this time around.

In my 5 year valuation, my earnings growth rate estimate for BA is 8%, now, that's a little bit higher than the consensus estimate of 5%; In my opinion however, their impressive backlog deserves a higher estimate of growth than 5%.

After extrapolating those earnings out over the next five years and then applying a multiple of 45, due to their backlog as well as me being generous, I am led to a target price of $349.33 in 5 years. A horribly subpar value that represents just 43% upside potential. I require 80% or more for the stock to be considered for my portfolios, and with where Boeing is today, they just aren't cutting it.

As a professional money manager over the last 25 years, I have looked at a lot of stock charts. I currently look at about 500 one-year charts per day. Are they in an uptrend, sideways trend, downtrend, or a leveling off trend? Are they breaking out or breaking down? Where are the current support and resistance lines?

Boeing has been in an uptrend that looks to be leveling off. Support is at $175, and resistance is at $250. I would call this a neutral chart at the current time.

In my Best Stocks Now app, BA is currently rated 1,759 out of 5,192 and is sitting at a Value grade of F and a Performance grade of C-, and that is just not good enough for a spot in our portfolio.

If you already own stock in BA, I would recommend a cautious but optimistic HOLD. Boeing has its largest backlog in history currently, and has no real competitors outside of Airbus (EADSF).

Ultimately, though, it's just not looking good enough for me to buy at the current time. While I am happy with the 22 positions that I currently own in this portfolio, I am still looking for seven more positions to fill it up, however. Any suggestions?

data.title: Honeywell Q4 Earnings Review: Margin Expansion Is Just Beginning
data.description:
Reviewing Honeywell's Earnings Results

Honeywell International Inc. (HON), a $138-billion market cap conglomerate, reported its Q4 earnings update yesterday. They basically beat only on the adjusted EPS (by 2.14%), missing the revenue consensus by 1.62% or ~$161 million (that was the first miss since mid-2024). The portfolio transformations and the costs of spinning off businesses in 2026 have mostly led to negative revisions on both the top and bottom lines (especially for the nearest Q1 2026 quarter):

But the stock price closed 4.89% higher after the print. Why?

JPM analysts wrote that HON's messy 2026 EPS profile will be cleared with the guide, creating an opportunity ahead of the looming break-up, on which the bank expects to get more clarity on many key fronts in the next 6 months. That's the reason for their upgrade and the rise of PT.

What the market seemingly decided to focus on, in addition to just the headline figures, was HON's massive 23% surge in organic orders and a record backlog exceeding $37 billion.

Particularly in long-cycle sectors like Aerospace and Building Automation, we finally got more sales growth visibility thanks to the trends in backlog. Also, the Aerospace spin-off timeline was pulled forward to Q3 2026 - that was disclosed at the earnings call. It told the market participants that HON has been progressing faster than was initially expected.

Now specifically coming to the aerospace question, right now, I will admit that we are so heavily focused to make spin happen in Q3, we'll share the specifics of stranded costs, et cetera, during our Investor Day coming up in June. But we are absolutely confident and committed that we will eliminate stranded costs in 12 to 18 months' time. Earlier, the better, we absolutely get it. But that's the range we expect to take it out.

Source: HON's Q4 Earnings call

In addition to that, the "real operating metrics" like organic sales growth of 11% and especially FCF trends (+48% YoY) were quite healthy in Q4, so it couldn't be ignored.

The guidance for FY2026 appears to be constructive, with a revenue range expected at $38.8-39.8 billion (that's ~$280 million below Wall Street's $39.58 billion consensus view), implying an organic growth of just 3-7%. During transitional periods like the one HON is going through now, it's quite normal, in my opinion. On the positive side, the profitability should generally improve as HON's management targets segment margins expanding 20 to 60 bps to a 22.7-23.1% range in FY2026. Effectively, that should be enough to offset the 30-basis-point headwind from an incremental $100 million investment in HON's Quantinuum unit.

The firm's consolidated FCF generation should stay at solid levels and expand by 4-10% YoY in FY2026, which will provide enough liquidity for further debt reduction before the spin-off in Q3 2026.

Honewell's Underappreciated Future

By the second half of this year, HON won't be a four-segment behemoth anymore as its Aerospace Technologies division will spin off and start to trade under the ticker (HONA) by Q3. In addition to that, they'll complete the separation of their Automation business. As a result, the market will face 3 standalone industry leaders: Honeywell Aerospace, Honeywell Automation, and the already-spun Solstice Advanced Materials.

The main positive from that move should be seen in the PostCo's financials. After exiting the loss-making and lower-margin Productivity Solutions and Services (PSS) and Warehouse and Workflow Solutions (WWS), Honeywell should have better margins in 2027-2028 - at least that's my understanding. So far, the market is failing to reflect that - after the faster-than-expected progress on the spin-off that we saw in Q4, the EPS revisions for 2027-2030 haven't really changed for the better.

One more thing that might positively impact HON's margins by 2027 is Honeywell's plans to make Quantinuum, its 54%-owned quantum unit, a public entity (likely by the end of 2026).

If they get the $10 billion expected valuation for it, it's going to unlock ~$5.4 billion in value for HON, and they'll be able to deleverage faster (thus, reducing the interest payments and boosting the EPS growth). From the current consensus estimates, that's not seen yet.

Another underappreciated catalyst that might materialize after the business separation is the firm's pivot toward AI-enabled autonomous solutions. HON has a deep domain experience and a vast installed base to drive its industrial transition from automation to autonomy relatively quickly. With their strategic partnership with Google (GOOG) that started in 2024, they can now integrate next-generation AI models and systems into industrial data to streamline autonomous operations.

With more AI-driven processes/operations, the margins should naturally pick up throughout the next few years, in my understanding.

Valuation Promises Some Upside Potential

I believe with a simplified corporate structure, HON shareholders can better capitalize on the existing "valuation gap" with some similar-sized peers such as RTX Corporation (RTX). JPM's analysts wrote in their update on HON that the currently visible disconnect should narrow thanks to its world-class aerospace business, with an absence of the "conglomerate discount".

I don't expect the gap to fade next year, though.

If I put a historically normal premium on the FY2027 EPS growth projection of 0.50%, and assume that HON adjusts toward a 24-25x earnings multiple by that time, I'll get a 12-month price target of ~$282.67/share.

$11.48 * 1.005 * 24.5x = $282.67/share (+24.39% upside)

Source: The author's calculations

With that in mind, I think HON deserves a "Buy" rating.

Where Can I Be Wrong?

It's always quite risky to buy companies in the middle of their business transition cycles because one can't be sure that the execution will be intact. So far, I like HON's developments on the separation front. However, I agree with the skeptics that some dissynergies might emerge that the bulls don't even think exist. Plus, there will be additional costs that may take years to prove they were actually reasonably spent.

Some of the catalysts and themes that I believe should help HON's rally in 2026 may turn out to be insignificant. For example, let's take the Quantinuum prospective IPO. We can't know if it goes public in 2026 (or in 2027-2028). It's currently running on "hype" with widening losses due to massive R&D and capital burn, so investors might require a cut to the expected valuation of $10 billion. For FY2026, the management plans an incremental $100 million YoY increase in investment for Quantinuum, and these capital injections may not lead to any profitable results for shareholders in the foreseeable future.

The Bottom Line

Despite the risks, I like HON's value proposition after the business separation. They're not just breaking down businesses to get cash and pay it out to shareholders - I see great logic behind the latest corporate steps. In my opinion, HON should eventually close the gap with RTX's valuation. If that happens, the upside potential from the current stock price might exceed 24%. Based on all that, I decided to rate HON as a "Buy".

Thank you for reading!

data.title: New Oriental Education: A 'Buy' With Beat And Raise Quarter (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I've become a bull on New Oriental Education (EDU) (9901.HK).

The company's 2QFY2026 (YE May 31) net income exceeded the market's expectations by a wide margin. It also revised the full year's sales guidance upwards.

EDU is still buying back its own shares. That's supportive of a potential 5+% capital management yield (cash returned/market cap).

Its 4QFY25 outperformance and worse-than-anticipated FY26 outlook were detailed in my prior July 31, 2025 update.

I'm Positively Surprised By Q2 Earnings

The firm released its recent three-month results on Wednesday, Jan 28.

EDU's Sep-Nov '25 normalized bottom-line was +69% higher year-on-year at $73M. That's also 31% ahead of the sell-side's projection as per S&P Capital IQ.

A faster pace of growth and enhanced profitability contributed to its strong showing.

The company's YoY top-line increase improved from 1QFY26's +6% to 2QFY26's +15%. The absolute figure of $1.19B was above the consensus's $1,163M estimate. Note that the split by business isn't disclosed.

Its adult/university- and college-related divisions' revenues rose 13% and 14%, respectively in the latest quarter. But the "New Educational Business Initiatives/NEBI" segment was the clear stand-out.

EDU highlighted at its Q2 briefing that "student enrollment" and "retention rates" for NEBI's "non-academic tutoring" operations benefited from "better word of mouth." I think that the optimization of user acquisition tactics was successful. This unit's 2QFY2026 turnover went up +22%.

Separately, the group's EBIT-to-sales widened by +4.7ppts (percentage points) YoY to 7.5% for this fiscal year's 2nd quarter. This is 200bps (basis points) ahead of the analysts' expectations.

You would have thought that EDU needed more advertising spend and aggressive capacity build-up to do well. That wasn't the case.

Its earnings call commentary indicated that "we slowed down the learning center expansion from 20%, 30% the year before" to FY26's targeted +10%. Also, 2QFY26 "Selling & Marketing/S&M" costs amounting to $0.19B represented a 1% contraction on YoY terms.

Management Is More Upbeat About Full-Year Prospects Now

The mid-point of EDU's expected FY26 turnover growth has been lifted from +7.5% to +10.0%.

In the preceding section, I outlined the solid top-line performances of its NEBI, adult-university, and college units.

The only weak spots in Q2 were "Overseas Test Preparation/OTP" and "Overseas Study Consulting Services/OSCS." Both of them delivered a low-single digit YoY sales increase during this period.

But I'm predicting a turnaround in 2HFY26.

The enterprise shared at the results meeting that "we (recently) merged the overseas test prep and consulting business."

Chinese students with study plans abroad will have to take English-language examinations and also seek advice on other admission-related matters. You can imagine that EDU wasn't able to meet their needs effectively by operating OSCS and OTP separately in the past.

Going forward, I think the firm can hold on to clients for longer by using initial test-prep solutions to cross-sell ongoing advisory services. That should translate into higher overseas-related educational revenues.

Also, EDU's analyst briefing remarks also stressed that "we are quite optimistic about the margin expansion in the second half of this year." The consensus FY2026 EBIT-to-revenue forecast is 12.4%, implying a 110bps improvement.

I believe the OTP-OSCS combination is likely to lead to a decrease in labor expenses. This is evidenced by the CFO's 2Q call comment that "one person can do more jobs even stronger than before."

Eyes On Mid-Single Digit Stockholder Yield

EDU is making healthy progress with its existing $0.3B buyback plan. That's in effect for a year beginning in Oct '25.

It spent $86M to purchase 1.6M of its "American Depositary Shares/ADS" in the last three months. It's equivalent to almost three-tenths of the entire program. This gives me confidence that the stock can realize a forward buyback-to-market cap of 3.2%.

The group has also committed to a cash payout of $1.2/ADS for the current fiscal year. It implies a 2.0% dividend-to-price ratio.

In my opinion, EDU's potential shareholder yield of 5.2% (2.0+3.2) will help to attract income-focused investors.

You Should Contemplate These Bear-Case Scenarios

The firm's NEBI division might cede ground to rivals, if its "word-of-mouth" marketing approach is executed poorly.

Demand for its OSCS/OTP offerings could weaken in the future. This assumes interest in foreign studies wanes.

The company may fail to complete its repurchase program. A diversion of capital to M&As is a possibility.

The Final Word

I'm changing my rating for this name from 'Hold' to 'Buy'.

EDU trades at a next twelve months' P/E of 17x. The same metric for its listed comparable TAL Education (TAL) is 23 times.

The stock boasts an upside of +35% (23/17). I've identified re-rating triggers such as sustained buybacks and the achievement of its full-year guide.

data.title: GDXJ: Gold Miners Are Making Massive Profits, With Prospects Of Strong Returns In 2026
data.description:
An unprecedented surge in gold prices has enabled miners to make gigantic profits last year. A blistering profit growth has also been translating into substantial returns for shareholders. VanEck Junior Gold Miners ETF (GDXJ) delivered a price return of 203% in the last twelve months with 137% increase in dividend payments. Given the strong demand for gold and other precious metals, I believe the uptrend is likely to continue in 2026. Therefore, I initiate coverage of GDJX with a buy rating.

Gold Price Surge is Likely to Continue in 2026

After generating a whopping 62% price increase in the previous year, Gold prices kicked off 2026 with a solid surge of more than 20% in the first month of the year. The precious metal achieved a record high of $5300 per ounce. The market pundits expect gold to mark new records in fiscal 2026 with bullish year end forecasts hovering in a range of $6,000 per ounce. A continued demand for the precious metal is likely to be fuelled by central banks aggressive buying, waning interest in US dollar, and geopolitical tensions.

In particular, China is playing a key role in pushing the prices higher amid its strategy of increasing gold reserves while lowering its dependence on US dollar. As of the end of 2025, the People's Bank of China accumulated 74.14 million fine troy ounce of gold. The bank’s gold buying increased in the past 14 consecutive months.

Similarly, other central banks are also seeking to increase their gold reserves. For example, as of the end of 2025, Poland's central bank’s gold reserves were around 550 tons. The bank’s Governor Adam Glapinski plans to boost gold reserves to 700 tons.

Besides the central banks gold buying strategy, the geopolitical tensions have also been adding to the precious metal’s demand. Increasing tensions between US and Europe over Greenland, the potential war with Iran and continued risk of tariffs are adding to safe-haven demand.

Whereas, Silver, which Goldman Sachs analyst believes is behaving like 'gold squared' or 'gold on steroids, rallied 240% in the last twelve months. The analyst further anticipates that this trend is likely extend until silver attains its historical standards compared to the gold. Silver’s price rally was supported speculative interest and strong physical demand. Overall, gold and silver market fundamentals appear strong to extend the uptrend in fiscal 2026.

It’s Time to Capitalize on Gold’s Price Growth

As the gold prices are rallying, it is a great time to capitalize on the potential uptrend. One of the best ways to make the most out of the precious metal’s upside is by investing directly in mining companies. This strategy can also enable investors to capitalize on silver’s upside because several gold companies are also involved in silver mining.

However, a single-stock investment carries a higher risk factor. Therefore, considering gold-focused ETFs can help investors to lower a risk factor while capitalizing on the broader market trends.

VanEck Junior Gold Miners ETF could be a worth conditioning investment vehicle. GDXJ's share price appreciated around 203% in the last twelve months, significantly higher than Gold’s spot price surge of 97%. Its price surge also outshined prominent large-cap gold-focused ETFs, including SPDR Gold Shares (GLD) and iShares Gold Trust (IAU).

GDXJ’s blistering price performance is supported by its exposure to both gold and silver mining companies. In addition, investors growing interest in small-caps in easing market conditions is contributing to its price upside. A low interest rate increases small caps potential to boost their investments in growth opportunities.

GDXJ’s portfolio comprises 96 stocks with the top 10 making up 42% of its portfolio weight. All of its top 10 holdings experienced a sharp share price surge in the last twelve months amid the robust price trend and profitability growth in the precious metals markets.

For example, Pan American Silver Corp (PAAS), GDXJ’s largest stock holding, experienced a share price surge of more than 180% in the last twelve months. The company’s earnings per share is expected to increase more than 170% year over year in 2025. Its earnings per share is also forecasted to grow by 60% in fiscal 2026. Whereas, Coeur Mining’s (CDE) share price rallied 286% in the last twelve months amid its substantial earnings growth power. The company is likely to deliver 409% earnings growth in 2025 and 90% in 2026.

Alamos Gold (AGI), Equinox Gold Corp (EQX) and Royal Gold (RGLD) are also among GDXJ’s top five holdings. Alamos Gold is expected to deliver 65% earnings growth in 2025 and 87% in 2026. The company’s stock price rallied 105% in the last twelve months. Equinox Gold has also made several financial and production records in the latest reported quarter. The company stated that with the ramp up of its Greenstone and Valentine assets, its production is likely to increase in 2026.

Royal Gold expects more than 50% year over year earnings growth in fiscal 2025 and 2026. The company’s share price rallied 110% in the last twelve months. In the latest reported quarter, gold contributed 78% to its record revenue of $252 million while silver and copper represented 12% and 7%.

The rest of GDXJ’s top 10 holdings include Hecla Mining (HL), First Majestic Silver Corp (AG), Evolution Mining (CAHPF) and Iamgold Corp (IAG). All these companies also reported a blistering share price surge in the last twelve months due to their massive revenue and earnings growth power. For example, Hecla Mining’s share price surged almost 380% in the last twelve months while First Majestic Silver Corp, and Iamgold Corp experienced a price increase of 354% and 248%, respectively.

GDXJ Characteristics

In addition to a solid price surge, VanEck Junior Gold Miners ETF offers a solid dividend yield of nearly 1.76%. GDXJ lifted its dividend by a massive 137% in the last twelve months. It has increased its dividends in the last three consecutive years. Dividend return contributed 7% to its total return of 210% in the last twelve months. GDXJ’s dividend growth prospects are high for 2026 as gold and silver companies are poised to generate a significant profit growth.

Moreover, despite a strong share price surge, GDXJ's trailing price to earnings ratio of 27 is in-line with the S&P 500. The fund’s expense ratio of 0.51% is slightly high compared to the median of all ETFs of 0.50%. Moreover, GDXJ’s liquidity is healthy with more than 5.7 million in average shares trading volume in the past three months.

Risk Factors to Consider

Gold is one of the most volatile precious metals with a strong correlation to the broader economic, political and monetary trends. Geopolitical stability and sustainable economic growth can negatively impact its price. The central banks higher concentration on the US dollar could also hurt gold's performance. A strong stock market bull run can also negatively impact safe-heaven assets. GDXJ’s standard deviation is around 35 and annualized price volatility is above 40%. Both metrics are up significantly from the median of all ETFs.

In Conclusion

Gold and other precious metals are expected to be the top performers in 2026, given geopolitical conflicts and economic uncertainty. The central banks across the world are also expected to boost their gold reserves. From an investment prospective, investment vehicles, such as VanEck Junior Gold Miners ETF, could enable investors make the most out of the uptrend. GDXJ generated blistering returns in 2025 due to its portfolio exposure to mid and small cap gold and silver companies from across the world.

data.title: Buy The Molina Dip: CMS Proposes Flat Medicare Advantage Increases For 2027
data.description:
Introduction

I first wrote about Molina Healthcare (MOH) in December 2025 highlighting the business dynamics and why I see the company as a strong investment opportunity amid weak market sentiment - you can read that article here. The reason for revisiting Molina is due to the sell-off across sector this week after the Centres for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) proposed lower than expected rate increases for Medicare Advantage (MA) plans in 2027. CMS's final ruling is expected in April, analysts had been expecting a ~5% rate increase while the proposal is for 0.09% (essentially flat).

Many investors bought the healthcare insurance dip in 2025, after higher utilisation, climbing medical costs and falling profitability. Investors expect 2026 to be an adjustment year, due to rapid rate increases across various programmes. However, this news relating to government programs like Medicare caused a sell-off across the sector. The message to the market - rate increases aren't as high as you expected.

Rhetoric from Trump over the years implies a very strong stance against health insurers and pharmaceutical giants alike. Overall my thesis on Molina remains mostly unchanged. Molina's Medicare exposure is 266,000 patients as of Q3, equivalent to 4.7% of their total plan members.

At the segment level, Medicare accounts for 14.5% of premium revenue and 14.6% of medical margin profit for the 9 months ending September 30th.

This is a relatively small segment and thus Molina's profits will be mostly protected with maybe some slight margin pressure at group level if the 0.09% 2027 rate increase gets approved. Overall I believe the core thesis (higher medicaid rates = lower group MCR = profit recovery) remains in tack. If the proposed MA rate changes stand, MCR for Molina's Medicare business will climb in 2027 but as a smaller segment impact is limited.

Sector Reaction

UnitedHealth (UNH) took the largest hit opening down around 19% intraday on the 27th of January, while other health insurers saw less extreme drops including Molina which is currently down 4.6% this week.

The market’s reaction appears more sentiment driven than fundamental, reflecting a broad reassessment of regulatory risk across all government-exposed insurers rather than a Molina-specific deterioration. The health insurance sector as a whole has taken hit after hit over the past 12 months, but the health insurance business model is not structurally broken.

The main concern of the street and the market is what happens if the U.S. government becomes increasingly hostile towards health insures (even the less aggressive coders like - Molina) by refusing justified rate increases across medicaid. Medicaid plans is the bulk of Molina's business and capitation-rate increases are set and agreed independently with each state annually.

Premiums must be actuarially sound as defined by CMS who can approve or reject capitation rate changes. These are done annually (different times for each state) and premiums lag as pricing for 2026 is based on 2025/24 data. Ultimately, if states propose rates which results in insurers being underpaid, insurers will pull out of managed care programs thereby deteriorating healthcare coverage. State representatives would being exposing themselves to backlash in this unlikely scenario.

From a structural system standpoint, insurers in medicaid are playing catch-up and operators like Molina are highly likely to experience profit recovery over the coming 24 months.

Valuation Snapshot

Let's assume the market is willing to pay a 8% FCF yield for Molina. In other words the company could be trading at a market cap to FCF of 12.5x, which reflects the volatility in earnings and positive drift of claims inflation which leads to higher premiums (higher revenue = higher profits long term). A multiple in the low teens is justifiable, given the high-barriers to entry and modest growth rate.

Ignoring Molina's negative net debt for a minute, just using sell-side FCF estimates and a valuation exercise (12.5x FCF), we can see the intrinsic value per share rises dramatically over the coming years. In the example above I'm using a current share price of $187. This assumes zero buybacks which management can comfortably do given their cash war chest of $8.45bn (debt is only $3.66bn). Management repurchased 2.8 million shares in Q3 utilising $500m of the $1bn buyback programme which runs until December 31st 2026. Management have likely drawn a portion of the remaining $500m authorisation in Q4 and into Q1.

If the sell-off caused by the proposed MA 2027 rate changes continues, management will probably seize the opportunity with more buybacks. At current levels another $1bn buyback programme (11.8% of cash), represents 5.32 million shares or 10% of shares outstanding.

Conclusion

The negative sentiment and regulatory uncertainty across U.S. health insurers has created what I believe is a meaningful mis-pricing in Molina shares. Molina shares are up 25% from my last article and I maintain my view that Molina is a "strong buy". While the proposed Medicare Advantage rate update for 2027 has understandably unsettled investors, its direct impact on Molina is limited given the company’s relatively small exposure to Medicare Advantage and its much larger reliance on Medicaid, where rate increases are negotiated at the state level.

This does not imply an immediate turnaround in 2026. Rather, Molina’s investment case rests on a gradual normalisation of medical cost ratios as Medicaid rate resets catch up with elevated utilisation. The key signal for investors over the next 12–18 months will be whether Molina can demonstrate improving Medicaid margins (MCR) as state pricing adjusts.

The proposed MA rate changes for 2027 is a small concern, investors will have to wait to see what CMS finalises in April. Last year CMS changed their MA price proposals from an initial 2.23% to 5.06%, more than double.

Therefore there is upside optionality if CMS revises Medicare Advantage rates higher in April, as it did last year, but even if they don't it doesn't break the Molina thesis. Medicare Advantage is a small area of the Molina group, which is highly exposed to Medicaid (75% of revenue) where rate increases are negotiated at the state-level. Although it would be good to see both ACA and Medicare lines perform well as this would translate to additional upside for investors.
